posts.post_content:
Your solo Communications Officer isn’t a unicorn.

They are not a miracle maker.

Yes, they’re outstanding.
Yes, a Jill-of-all-trades.

But it’s unfair to think a one-person comms team can pull off every job.

(Not without burning out.)

Even worse are the unrealistic expectations of a solo Director of Fundraising and Communications. That’s trying to milk two different departments out of one brave soul.

So nonprofit leaders, you have two options:

1️⃣ 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀
If you can only hire a generalist, your marketing won’t be world-class. No shame; that’s probably all you can afford. Just stop comparing your brand to big charities with big professionals. Your Comms Officer is doing an incredible job keeping all the balls in the air.

2️⃣ 𝗜𝗻𝘃𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗶𝗻 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁𝘀
If you don’t want to be second best at everything, put your money where your mouth is. Hire a copywriter. Hire a graphic designer. Hire a social media wiz. Etc. Then your Comms Manager can be an orchestra conductor, bringing all these specialists together well.

Choice is yours.

Because the overburdened Comms Officer is a systemic nonprofit issue. They’re some of the most talented and tireless (but tired!) people we meet.

Expecting one person to juggle it all?
Expecting massive fundraising?
Expecting a magical brand?

Time for a reality check.

Repost ♻️ if you agree.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofit leaders must stop expecting a single communications generalist to deliver world-class marketing and fundraising—either adjust expectations for what a one-person comms role can do or invest in specialists so the work gets done without burning out your staff.

posts.post_content:
Simple does not mean shallow.

Just look at this viral activist campaign. 👇🏽

Emanuele Morelli’s ad — “An empire built on waste” — confronts the pollution and human exploitation of fast fashion with a deep and wildly effective message.

But there are no words except for the brand name.

And campaigns like this worked, as France just passed a bill to regulate the fast fashion industry.

The lesson for your communications:

Want attention?
Don’t yell. 

Say less.
Mean more.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Restraint and visual simplicity—saying less but with deeper meaning—draw attention and can spur real change, so favor concise, powerful messaging over shouting.

posts.post_content:
What has aid ever done for anyone?

Absolutely nothing.

Except:

Educate kids.
Feed the hungry.
Vaccinate children.
Provide clean water.
Nearly eradicate polio.
Create financial growth.
Stop preventable deaths.
Bring peace & democracy.
Respond to natural disasters.
Save millions of lives and more.

At a time of dangerous aid cuts around the world, this blistering parody video lands a gut punch to every critic — from Musk to MAGA.

“Aid needs an overhaul, not an elimination,” says Unlock Aid.

Share ♻️ to shift the narrative.

Fund it. Fix it.
But never forget it.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Aid has delivered concrete, life‑saving and development results around the world, so at a time of dangerous cuts it should be defended, funded, and reformed—not eliminated.

posts.post_content:
Philanthropy lost its meaning.

Because money stole the story.

The word 𝙥𝙝𝙞𝙡𝙖𝙣𝙩𝙝𝙧𝙤𝙥𝙮 means love (𝙥𝙝𝙞𝙡) of humanity (𝙖𝙣𝙩𝙝𝙧𝙤𝙥𝙤𝙨). Not love of grantmaking. And people in many ‘poorer’ Global South countries give more than ‘rich’ Global North nations.

Just look at the World Giving Index. ⤵

🇮🇩 Indonesia tops the list seven straight years.
↳ Based on donating, volunteering, and helping strangers.

Low- & mid-income countries are half the top 20:
🇰🇪 Kenya
🇱🇷 Liberia
🇳🇬 Nigeria
🇬🇳 Guinea
🇺🇦 Ukraine
🇹🇭 Thailand
🇲🇾 Malaysia
🇲🇲 Myanmar
🇬🇲 The Gambia

Africa also boasts in the top 45:
🇱🇾 Libya
🇹🇩 Chad
🇬🇭 Ghana
🇪🇹 Ethiopia
🇸🇳 Senegal
🇸🇱 Sierra Leone

🇺🇸 Yet, USA is the sole ‘Western power’ in the top 10.

🇪🇺 Europe has only three in the top 15, fewer than Africa.

🇬🇧 The UK ranks just 22nd most generous (and trending downward).

👍🏾 Across the globe, immigrants are more likely to give than nationals.


Why does this matter?

Once again, we must shatter the illusion of Global South donor dependency.

“Sixteen years in the field have shown me that actual philanthropists … are much more prevalent among the regular population and are only rarely found inside the field’s formal institutions,” says Edgar Villanueva. 

In other words, many of us already practice real 𝘱𝘩𝘪𝘭-𝘢𝘯𝘵𝘩𝘳𝘰𝘱𝘰𝘴.

Helping out a stranger.
Sending remittances.
Donating small gifts.
Volunteering time.

All #philanthropy.

Because generosity isn’t a VIP club.
Cash isn’t the only ticket in.

So if we monetize the story of giving, we bankrupt its very soul.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Philanthropy has been distorted by focusing on money and formal institutions—true philanthropy is everyday generosity (often led by Global South and immigrant communities) and must be recognized beyond cash, Western narratives, or grantmaking.

posts.post_content:
USAID cuts $60 billion: 𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲.

Global South gives $590 billion: 𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱.

When aid budgets are being slashed around the world, the damaging mentality of some Westerners — “first they should help themselves” — is just wrong. Because the Global South already 𝘪𝘴 helping themselves.

Through remittances. 

These silent giants of giving donate more to families and communities than all other international aid.

Combined.

🏦 Like official development assistance: $180B
💷 Or cross-border philanthropy: $70B
💰 And private capital: $400M

To put this data in sharper focus, the Global South diaspora is (by far) the world’s top philanthropist. For itself.

But the Global North gets all the donor credit.

“This is one of the most under-reported and unexamined issues in global development,” says Gillian Marcelle, PhD.

It’s a wake-up call.

“It challenges the notion that we are beggars — even though aid plays a key role in our development, we are playing a much bigger role,” says Willson Chivhanga. “This knowledge allows us to reclaim and own our developmental agenda.”

Remittances are an unsung hero. 

Let’s make them a headliner in our narratives.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Remittances from the Global South already outscale official aid and quietly fund development, so we must stop treating recipients as passive and reframe narratives to recognize and elevate this self-help philanthropy.

posts.post_content:
“We do not pay for overhead.”

“Especially not your salary.”

This parody video, 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜𝗳 𝗣𝗶𝘇𝘇𝗮 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗪𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗙𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗡𝗼𝗻𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝘁𝘀, has gone viral over and over again. Because it painfully shows why nonprofit leaders need unrestricted funding to serve impact.

So we should share ♻️ this story over and over again too:

Overhead is operational, not optional.
Overhead is a virtue, not a vice.
Overhead is fuel, not fluff.

And “if you are restricting funding and focusing on overhead, you are actively preventing nonprofits from doing their work,” says Vu Le.

Let’s starve no mission.
Finance whole pies, not slices.
Invest in infrastructure, not ingredients.

(𝘝𝘪𝘥𝘦𝘰 𝘤𝘳𝘦𝘥𝘪𝘵: 𝘏𝘶𝘮𝘢𝘯 𝘚𝘦𝘳𝘷𝘪𝘤𝘦𝘴 𝘊𝘰𝘶𝘯𝘤𝘪𝘭 𝘰𝘧 𝘕𝘦𝘸 𝘠𝘰𝘳𝘬)

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors and funders should provide unrestricted funding for nonprofit overhead and infrastructure because overhead is essential to impact—restricting funds to program line items prevents organizations from doing their work.

posts.post_content:
30 conferences for your 2026 fundraising. 👇🏽

The event circuit begins next week.

And events are a great way to be findable, as a five-minute hallway conversation with a funder can beat six months of Zoom.

But you cannot shortcut 𝘧𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘢𝘣𝘪𝘭𝘪𝘵𝘺.

So if all you’re amplifying is a confusing message, no amount of conference handshakes, lanyards, or donor happy hours will rescue you.

Make yourself fundable.
Then go be findable.

Write the story.
Then take the room.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Before chasing conference visibility, make sure you have a clear, fundable story—being findable won't rescue confusing messaging.

posts.post_content:
Clear beats clever. 

But this viral fundraising campaign is both.

These brilliant ads for WWF show “The Hidden Cost” on wildlife from our everyday purchases. 

The most incredible part?

Nikolaj Lykke Viborg created this nonprofit campaign entirely in ChatGPT. A powerful reminder that even small teams with small budgets can craft powerful communications.

Creativity turns heads.
Clarity turns donations.

Make your donors stop, think, fund.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Clear, creatively executed messaging—even produced with tools like ChatGPT—lets small nonprofit teams produce viral campaigns that convert attention into donations.

posts.post_content:
Data is worthless without a story.

If donors don’t get it, they won’t give.

It reminds me of opening a LEGO box as a kid and dumping the pieces on the table. All the parts are there, the potential is obvious, but nothing means anything yet.

And your #fundraising data is no different.

Too many nonprofits stop at how many people you reach or a percentage improvement. Yet “numbers numb, stories stimulate,” says Jeff Brooks.

Which makes me ask this every time I review a pitch now:

Have we built a complete picture?
Or, are we just handing donors a pile of parts?

One house beats a thousand bricks, every time.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Data alone won't move donors; nonprofits must organize and narrate their data into a complete, meaningful story that shows impact and motivates giving.

posts.post_content:
👇🏽 The ultimate theory of change cheat sheet.👇🏽

For your comms and #fundraising.

A classic logic model falls flat. And many theories of change are too complex with arrows, assumptions, academia — leaving donors confused.

But this magic one-pager helps you:

→ Raise money
→ Improve M&E
→ Inform staffing
→ Motivate teams
→ Clarify messaging
→ Fine-tune programs
→ Generate partnerships
→ Develop a strategic plan
→ Make the most of funding

Borrow this #infographic. Make it your own.

And turn theory into tangible funds.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: A simplified, one‑page theory of change infographic converts complex, academic logic models into a practical tool that drives fundraising and improves program, staffing, measurement, and messaging outcomes.

posts.post_content:
Ads sell with feelings.

NGOs sell with footnotes.

That’s the problem. Corporates persuade with one sharp line, while nonprofits often drown donors in detail.

What’s worse?

Our communications often forget the most important audience.

“Too often we speak about people, not with them,” says Johan Karlsson, who created this lighthearted parody. “If I put myself in the shoes of the ‘affected community’ — I’d never buy from a brand that talked about me this way.”

So stop writing like a grant application and start writing like a movement.

Clear.
Crisp.
Compelling.

That’s how we inspire our donors.
That’s how we respect our community.
That’s how we stay fundable and findable.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofits must stop writing like grant applications and instead use clear, emotional, movement-style messaging that speaks with communities to inspire donors and remain fundable.

posts.post_content:
Four of the most harmful words in fundraising:

Vulnerable, marginalized, at-risk & underserved.

These terms are all dangerous for the same reasons: they’re cliché, they’re deficit language, and they tell the donor nothing specific about your community.

Think about it. ⤵

People can be vulnerable, marginalized, at-risk, or underserved because of poverty, hunger, gender, climate, political oppression, displacement, poor health, lack of education, lack of clean water, lack of employment, lack of housing, lack of legal protection, and on and on and on.

What’s the solution then?

Use specific, plain language:

𝘼𝙩-𝙧𝙞𝙨𝙠 𝙘𝙝𝙞𝙡𝙙𝙧𝙚𝙣 → 𝙆𝙞𝙙𝙨 𝙣𝙤𝙩 𝙞𝙣 𝙨𝙘𝙝𝙤𝙤𝙡

𝙑𝙪𝙡𝙣𝙚𝙧𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 𝙬𝙤𝙢𝙚𝙣 → 𝙒𝙤𝙢𝙚𝙣 𝙨𝙚𝙚𝙠𝙞𝙣𝙜 𝙬𝙤𝙧𝙠

𝙐𝙣𝙙𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙚𝙧𝙫𝙚𝙙 𝙛𝙖𝙢𝙞𝙡𝙞𝙚𝙨 → 𝙁𝙖𝙢𝙞𝙡𝙞𝙚𝙨 𝙬𝙞𝙩𝙝 𝙖 𝙥𝙖𝙧𝙚𝙣𝙩 𝙞𝙣 𝙥𝙧𝙞𝙨𝙤𝙣

𝙈𝙖𝙧𝙜𝙞𝙣𝙖𝙡𝙞𝙯𝙚𝙙 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙢𝙪𝙣𝙞𝙩𝙞𝙚𝙨 → 𝘾𝙤𝙢𝙢𝙪𝙣𝙞𝙩𝙞𝙚𝙨 𝙬𝙞𝙩𝙝𝙤𝙪𝙩 𝙚𝙡𝙚𝙘𝙩𝙧𝙞𝙘𝙞𝙩𝙮


Sure, it’s a little longer, but it’s a lot stronger.

And here’s the tough love. If you think you’re solving countless different types of “vulnerability” in a single mission and can’t describe your people in plain language, that might mean you’re not focused enough.

The other nuance here?

Always list the people or communities first, so that 𝘸𝘩𝘰 they are defines them. Not 𝘸𝘩𝘢𝘵 they’re currently experiencing.

I’m not saying this is the perfect solution for every organization. But I am saying we can quit dancing around the terminology, avoid the deficit language, and just state the facts. 

Our donors will better understand.
And our communities will be better understood.

What terms would you add to this list?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Stop using vague deficit labels like 'vulnerable,' 'marginalized,' 'at‑risk,' and 'underserved' and instead use specific, people‑first plain language that names who you serve and what they lack so donors understand and your mission stays focused.

posts.post_content:
You might be telling the wrong story.

There are three types.
↳ But only one converts donors.

Notice the important nuance in this infographic.

“The first two stories at best hint at what their giving might do — and at worst are irrelevant to the donor,” says fundraising expert Jeff Brooks. “The story about the change that can happen when the donor gets involved raises a lot more funds.”

That’s why The Better Fundraising Company, which created this model, says the best donor solicitation is a classic before-and-after:

𝘙𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘵 𝘯𝘰𝘸 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘯𝘨𝘴 𝘢𝘳𝘦 𝘟, 𝘣𝘶𝘵 𝘪𝘧 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘥𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘵𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘺 𝘸𝘪𝘭𝘭 𝘣𝘦 𝘠.

So are you using the right narrative?

Not all stories are fundable.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Only the donor-centered, before-and-after story that clearly shows the change a gift will create actually converts donors, so fundraisers must use that narrative rather than vague or irrelevant stories.

posts.post_content:
Most of you will skip this post.

That’s a #fundraising mistake.

I know, I know: strategic planning isn’t the most exciting topic (people typically don’t engage as much on this topic). But a big donor once told me that the number one reason they don’t invest is when they don’t believe the organization can execute its vision. 

So if your theory of change is:

✔️ 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 you do
✔️ 𝗪𝗵𝘆 you exist
✔️ 𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 you’re going

Then your strat plan is the other bookend:

✔️ 𝗛𝗼𝘄 you’ll get it done
✔️ 𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 it will be done
✔️ 𝗪𝗵𝗼 will get it done

That’s it.

Download this #infographic to get 10 steps to show proof for fundraising.

Then turn theory into traction.
↳ From strategy to funding flow.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: To win donor investment you must pair your theory of change with a clear strategic plan that proves how, when, and who will execute it — then use that proof in your fundraising.

posts.post_content:
You should be paid more.

But nonprofits face an unjust rulebook. 

Because society discriminates against you when it comes to compensation, says Dan Pallotta in this classic video that’s more timely than ever.

As aid budgets have been cut.
Thousands of people are being laid off. 
Donors don’t allow fundraising to cover salaries.

And nonprofit teams are left sacrificing their own well-being, yet again.

Here’s why it matters. ⤵

“Nonprofits exist in a free market. They need to be competitive in order to attract the best talent. It’s that simple,” says Joan Garry. “A healthy nonprofit with outstanding leadership will be able to help more people in a more effective and bigger way for a much longer time.”

So compensate all changemakers fairly.

Underpaid = undermined impact.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofits must pay changemakers fairly because societal and donor-driven constraints on compensation are unjust and directly undermine organizations' ability to attract talent and sustain impact.

posts.post_content:
To get funding, be fundable and findable.

My keynote speech, on a single page.

Six laws.
One outcome.
Maximized funding.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: To maximize funding, organizations must be both fundable and findable — a concept Kevin distills into six guiding 'laws' that produce one outcome: maximized funding.

posts.post_content:
I’ve seen 450 pitches in the past nine years.

And most of you forget one big donor question:

𝙃𝙤𝙬 𝙖𝙧𝙚 𝙮𝙤𝙪 𝙙𝙞𝙛𝙛𝙚𝙧𝙚𝙣𝙩?

It’s the second-biggest complaint we hear: they just don’t get how you’re different from nonprofits they already fund. So once a donor understands what you do (the biggest issue!), tell them what makes you unique.

“It’s not enough to be the best at what you do; you must be perceived as the only one who does what you do,” says Jerry Garcia.

That’s your positioning strategy, that’s part of being findable, and that’s what this infographic is about.

So ♻️ repost to help your network.

Difference = donations.

Emphasize it.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: When pitching donors, clearly and explicitly communicate what makes your nonprofit uniquely different, because donors frequently ask 'How are you different?' and that perceived distinctiveness drives donations.

posts.post_content:
Other nonprofits are not your enemy.

There’s plenty of money out there.

In the United States alone, foundations have $1.5 trillion sitting in the bank.

So yes, position your brand.
Yes, tell donors why you’re unique.
Yes, use sharp communications to stand out.

But I believe that we’re all on the same team (friends, even?) and the scarcity mindset only keeps our #fundraising from scoring big. The boys in this video understood abundance.

One ball. Infinite joy.

That’s the energy our #nonprofit sector needs, now more than ever.

Do you agree?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofits should adopt an abundance mindset—collaborating rather than treating peers as enemies—because there’s ample philanthropic capital and scarcity thinking undermines fundraising success.

posts.post_content:
The 30 best conferences for your fundraising. 🚨

A great way to be findable in 2026.

Because events can be gold. During my book launch at The Sidebar/Skoll World Forum in April, I remember thinking that a five-minute hallway conversation with a funder can beat six months of Zoom.

But you cannot shortcut 𝘧𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘢𝘣𝘪𝘭𝘪𝘵𝘺.

Conferences are accelerants, not substitutes. So if all you’re amplifying is a confusing message, no amount of handshakes, lanyards, or donor happy hours will rescue you.

Make yourself fundable.
Then go be findable.

Write the story.
Then take the room.

💪🏽💛

P.S. Repost ♻️ this infographic, courtesy of Segal Family Foundation, to help a leader in need. Then which events would you add?
posts.core_message: Conferences can accelerate fundraising by making you findable, but they won't substitute for being fundable—clear messaging and readiness must come first.

posts.post_content:
A picture is worth a thousand donations.

Yet your fundraising is text, text, text, text, text.

“Never in the history of humanity have we vomited more words in more places with more velocity,” says Smart Brevity. “But people keep banging out emails, letters, memos, papers, stories and books like it’s 1980.”

That’s why this infographic stopped my scroll.
↳ A complex topic explained in 13 words.

It’s a reminder: we often don’t need more text on our website or more pages in our grant application or more slides in our pitch deck or more time speaking in our donor meetings.

We often need an illustration.

So make it visual or make it forgettable.

Visibility beats verbosity.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: In fundraising communications, prioritize clear, concise visuals that explain complex ideas quickly—visibility beats verbosity.

posts.post_content:
You only have five ways to raise more money:

1️⃣ Find new donors (𝙝𝙪𝙣𝙩𝙞𝙣𝙜)
2️⃣ Retain existing donors (𝙛𝙖𝙧𝙢𝙞𝙣𝙜)
3️⃣ Increase average gift size (𝙛𝙖𝙧𝙢𝙞𝙣𝙜)
4️⃣ Get donors to give more often (𝙛𝙖𝙧𝙢𝙞𝙣𝙜)
5️⃣ Grow word-of-mouth donor referrals (𝙛𝙖𝙧𝙢𝙞𝙣𝙜)

That’s it.

Donor hunting is expensive.
↳ Funder farming is much cheaper.

Hunting involves a lot of luck.
↳ Farming is mainly within your control.

Hunting is hard; every nonprofit is doing it.
↳ Farming is a lot easier; many leaders ignore it.

Hunting has fewer proven formulas.
↳ Farming flourishes via relationships and brand.

“Non-fundraisers tend to assume the only way to grow income is to add new donors to your donor pool. So there is a tendency at the Board and Senior Leadership level to put too much focus on the new,” says Michelle Benson.

The root of success here?

Fundraising is mostly retention (farming), not acquisition (hunting).

Less chasing donors.
More growing relationships.

Less aim and fire.
More plant and nurture.

Less kill.
More till.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: The most reliable, cost-effective way to grow nonprofit income is to focus on 'farming'—retaining and cultivating existing donors (bigger, more frequent gifts and referrals)—rather than primarily 'hunting' for new donors.

posts.post_content:
30% of nonprofits fail within 10 years.

The killer isn’t fundraising.

You’re more likely to die from mismanagement, lack of focus, no product/market fit, leadership issues, burnout, and trying to scale too quickly.

To name a few.

I learned this the hard way by building four organizations: one that grew to acquisition, one that failed, and two that achieved solid success.

So get 15 laws of startup success (most apply to bigger nonprofits too), adapted from Y Combinator.

And repost ♻️ this infographic to help your network.

What would you add to this list?
What do you question?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofits most often fail due to mismanagement, lack of focus, poor product/market fit, leadership issues and scaling mistakes—not fundraising—so leaders should adopt startup-tested principles (15 laws adapted from Y Combinator) to avoid those pitfalls.

posts.post_content:
Small nonprofits have superpowers.

So stop thinking you need millions first.

Because “a $500k organization is not just a smaller version of a $5M organization,” says Rebecca White. “When you compare yourself to someone raising money in a different ecosystem, you miss the practical advantages right in front of you.”

Here’s the twist. ⤵

“Truly new things rarely need an abundance of money,” says Kevin Kelly. “If that was so, billionaires would have a monopoly on inventing new things, and they don’t. Instead, almost all breakthroughs are made by those who lack money.” 

That’s why I loved how The Bridgespan Group recently listed 𝙎𝙚𝙫𝙚𝙣 𝘼𝙨𝙨𝙚𝙩𝙨 𝙤𝙛 𝙎𝙢𝙖𝙡𝙡 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝙈𝙞𝙙𝙨𝙞𝙯𝙚 𝙉𝙤𝙣𝙥𝙧𝙤𝙛𝙞𝙩𝙨.

Reframe small as superpower. Glass half full, not half empty.

Which of these 7 resonate with you?

Which do not?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Small nonprofits should reframe their size as a strategic advantage—limited money can spur innovation and practical strengths that are missed when comparing yourself to larger organizations.

posts.post_content:
100,000 followers! 🥳

I’m sitting here smiling and soaking it in.

Because I started writing on LinkedIn just three years ago with 2,800 of you early followers and a simple idea: share insights we’re learning at MIGHTY ALLY to democratize this fundraising expertise.

So that every leader can be fundable and findable.

After 27 million impressions and a bestselling book, scroll right for my lessons on how to grow YOUR own thought leadership following too.

But here’s the bummer. ⤵

Most of you aren’t seeing my posts due to the AI-slop filling your LinkedIn feed. So click that 🛎️ bell on my profile to get alerted. Then:

👍🏽 𝗟𝗜𝗞𝗘 every post real quick
⬇️ 𝗦𝗔𝗩𝗘 by clicking the three dots

It takes you just two seconds, but tells the algorithm you want more. And if you think a post is really worth it:

♻️ 𝗥𝗘𝗣𝗢𝗦𝗧 to help your network
💬 𝗖𝗢𝗠𝗠𝗘𝗡𝗧 so we learn together

I’m beyond grateful for all 100,000 of you.

Let’s keep building a world where every nonprofit is…

Fundable.
Findable.
Funded.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Celebrating 100,000 followers, Kevin asks supporters to take a few quick engagement actions (click the bell, like, save, repost, comment) to overcome AI-clutter and amplify his nonprofit fundraising thought leadership so every organization can be fundable, findable, and funded.

posts.post_content:
You don’t need more data in your fundraising.

Stop stuffing your proposals with pie charts.

Why?

In an aid-effectiveness study, thousands of German donors said that testimonials resonate more than data.

And 56% of social media users who donate online said compelling storytelling makes them give most. Even savvy trusts and foundations first decide based on emotion, then justify with facts later.

Don’t get me wrong. ⤵

Investing in measurement is simply the right thing to do for impact accountability. So narrative + numbers is the fundraising combo. 

But “people forget facts; they never forget a good story,” says Claire Axelrad, J.D., CFRE

In fundraising, memory is money. 

Numbers tell.
Stories sell.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Fundraising should lead with emotional storytelling to make donors remember and give, using data only to measure impact and justify decisions afterward.

posts.post_content:
I’ve never seen so many leaders scared.

Aid cuts. Funding fears.

2026 could be a repeat of last year. But the top 100 UK foundations have more than £72 billion sitting in the bank.

So use this new wishlist for your #fundraising.

Then repost ♻️ for someone in need.

Be fundable.
Be findable.
Be unstoppable.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Despite widespread fears about aid cuts and funding, nonprofits should act now to become fundable, findable, and unstoppable — leveraging the large pool of foundation capital (top 100 UK foundations hold £72bn) instead of panicking.

posts.post_content:
More fundraising can’t fix your fundraising.

But these 17 resolutions can. 👇🏽

Of course you need to fundraise, eventually. But if your income has stalled, stop what you’re doing. Stop chasing donations. Gather your leadership team and make the resolution:

To get funding, 𝗪𝗘 𝗪𝗜𝗟𝗟 be fundable and findable.

Then ♻️ repost for a leader in need.

Need help?

Get my book 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 with dozens of action step exercises plus 33 illustrations, including tools and templates for you to use:

fundablefindable.org

This is the shift.
Make it stick.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: If your income has stalled, stop merely chasing donations and instead commit as an organization to becoming deliberately fundable and findable—use these 17 resolutions (and the accompanying book/tools) to make that shift and sustain it.

posts.post_content:
Overhead is fuel. Not fluff.
Overhead is a virtue. Not a vice.
Overhead is pivotal. Not peripheral.
Overhead is operational. Not optional.
Overhead is dynamite. Not deadweight.
Overhead is investment. Not indulgence.

So overhead 𝘤𝘢𝘯 make a lousy charity good.

Like the 35 essentials (in the comments below) donors should invest in.

Yet nonprofits actually lose money when you categorize some of these items as indirect/overhead rather than as direct costs/program-related.

“What’s more important is the practice of considering whether an expense advances your impact,” advises Jenna Rogers-Rafferty, MIA. “If you can justify that it does with a straight face to your funder, you should consider it a direct cost and feel empowered to defend that decision.”

And let’s not perpetuate the overhead myth.

“It’s time for a change,” says Vu Le. “Stop furthering the idea that overhead is bad. Operating, administrative, and fundraising expenses are necessary for us to do effective work.”

Repost ♻️ to shift the narrative.

Because myths hold power only if we let them.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Overhead is an essential, impact-enabling investment that should be reframed and defended as direct, program-advancing spending rather than stigmatized as wasteful overhead.

posts.post_content:
This meme? 😂

Your strategy last year? 😬

Now that it’s 2026, don’t forget to revisit your annual goals every three-months to set new, quarterly priorities.

Annual goals are never static.
Strategic plans are never static.

“The worst thing that ever happened to strategy was that it got jammed together with 𝘱𝘭𝘢𝘯,” says Kevin Starr at Mulago Foundation. “If your strategy is frozen in a plan, then when that plan goes to hell, your strategy goes with it.”

Starr continues. ⤵

“Your strategy has got to be an independent, living thing that responds to changing circumstances and new information. It should be reviewed often, and the bias should be toward change.”

Because quarterly clarity beats annual chaos.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Treat your strategy as a living, adaptable system by revisiting annual goals every quarter so plans stay responsive instead of frozen and obsolete.

posts.post_content:
Boards and teams hate raising money.

But they do already, whether they like it or not.

Because the majority of fundraising is just communications. And every single email, meeting, event, field visit, or social post is like a mini donor campaign. So the entire team and board is a fundraising touchpoint.

Get this. ⤵

“Not everyone on your team is going to be confident or comfortable with asking, but that doesn’t mean they don’t play important roles in fundraising,” says Darian Rodriguez Heyman.

Learn the three roles in this quick guide.
↳ Click full screen to download the PDF.

Every voice is a funding force.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Fundraising is largely communications, so every team and board member—whether they ask or not—serves as a fundraising touchpoint and should be activated into defined roles to amplify giving.

posts.post_content:
Annual panic starts today.

Suddenly everyone’s scrambling:

Team retreats thrown together, annual plans rushed, 2026 strategies built like sandcastles against the tide.

And this is where things go wrong.

No wonder a major family foundation once told me the number one reason they don’t invest: it’s not the idea, it’s the plan. Or lack of one.

But breathe. It’s just December 1st, not doomsday.

So get the 7 DOs & DON’Ts for your annual planning. Then walk into the new year fundable, findable, and investable.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Don’t succumb to end-of-year panic and rushed annual plans—pause, use the 7 DOs & DON’Ts, and you’ll enter the new year with a fundable, findable, and investable plan.

posts.post_content:
Every thought leader gets hate.

Fundraisers take punches too.

So I lean on these five lessons from Tim Ferriss in my own thought leadership journey. They’ll keep your voice steady when the critics get loud, from negative donors to picky community members to online trolls.

1️⃣ It doesn’t matter how many people don’t get it. What matters is how many people do.

2️⃣ 10% of people will find a way to take anything personally. Expect it and treat it as math.

3️⃣ You can’t reason someone out of something they didn’t reason themselves into.

4️⃣ To avoid criticism, do nothing, say nothing, and be nothing.

5️⃣ Trying to get everyone to like you is a sign of mediocrity.

Haters hate.
Leaders create.

Let pushback fuel your growth, not your doubt.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Criticism is inevitable for thought leaders and fundraisers, so treat negative responses as expected 'math,' focus on those who understand your work, and let pushback fuel growth rather than doubt.

posts.post_content:
A bad proposal or grant application is like:

Serving a gourmet meal on a paper plate.
Painting a masterpiece on torn canvas.
Presenting diamonds in a plastic bag.

It’s practically giving donors a reason to say no.

So avoid these seven deadly sins that cost you fundraising and sales. Instead, use the 𝙉𝙊𝙎𝙀 formula in your next proposal. I’ve used this exact format to generate more than $4.5 million here at Mighty Ally.

And I’m surprised how few leaders know it.

Slide right for a 90-second guide. ⤵

Then make it compelling.
Or lose the funding.
posts.core_message: Poorly presented proposals give donors reasons to say no—avoid seven common mistakes and use my NOSE formula (which generated $4.5M at Mighty Ally) to make your next proposal compelling and win funding.

posts.post_content:
The word 𝙚𝙢𝙥𝙤𝙬𝙚𝙧 is a problem for three reasons.

And it’s hurting your fundraising.

1️⃣ The term is generic and cliche. Millions of nonprofits use it, so you sound like everyone else. Remember, difference = donations.

2️⃣ It’s a substitute for a stronger verb that’s more specific to your model, like 𝘴𝘩𝘦𝘭𝘵𝘦𝘳 or 𝘵𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘯 or 𝘧𝘶𝘯𝘥 or 𝘵𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘵 𝘥𝘦𝘱𝘳𝘦𝘴𝘴𝘪𝘰𝘯 instead of empower.

3️⃣ It carries a savior vibe; you must have power to empower another. Especially an issue for expats working in communities not their own.

“While the term ‘empowerment’ is used with the best intentions, describing all kinds of not-for-profit work this way can unintentionally reinforce the inequalities we aim to address,” says Paul Hunt.

Look, I get it.

Many of you communicate in a second or third language, or you’re not a creative writer. So there’s no shame if you currently use the term. 

But it’s easy to fix. And I’m glad to help you. ⤵

Comment with your current language and I’ll empower you.

See what I did there? 
See how the word is confusing?

Comment with your current language and I’ll 𝘣𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘴𝘵𝘰𝘳𝘮 𝘰𝘱𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘴 𝘸𝘪𝘵𝘩 you.

Stop empowering.
Start getting specific.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: The vague term 'empower' damages fundraising because it's cliché, replaces more specific action verbs tied to your work, and can sound savior-like—so stop using it and substitute concrete, program-specific verbs (and I’ll help you brainstorm replacements).

posts.post_content:
100,000 of you follow me.

But only 8,000 of you follow our company page.

And my personal Fundable & Findable newsletter has 15,000 subscribers after two years, while our MIGHTY ALLY newsletter has 4,000 after nine years — even though it’s all the same mission.

What’s the lesson for you?

Your personal thought leadership can be as powerful for fundraising as your organizational brand.

It’s a one-two combination.

Because “people buy people,” says Chris J Reed. “People follow people, not companies.”

Same goes for donors.

Reed calls it the Richard Branson effect, where 100 times more people follow the leader of Virgin than the company itself.

And I can think of many examples where individuals built movements through their own thought leadership (not just their nonprofit):

→ Wawira Njiru
→ Fred Swaniker
→ Dr. Paul Farmer
→ Safeena Husain
→ Malala Yousafzai
→ Bryan Stevenson
→ Muhammad Yunus
→ Jacqueline Novogratz

Don’t just take my word for it. ⤵

“The CEO is the person the board looks to for vision, donors look to for conviction, and the community looks to for hope,” says Michelle Flores Vryn, CFRE. “The job, at its core, is carrying the story of why this organization exists.”

So be fundable as an organization.
𝘼𝙣𝙙 be findable as a human.

Your organization needs a brand.
𝘼𝙣𝙙 your donors need you.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofit leaders should build their personal thought leadership alongside their organization's brand because individual visibility attracts donors and strengthens fundraising more effectively than relying on the organizational brand alone.

posts.post_content:
New year’s mantra for you:

Anything is possible, but everything isn’t.

Not enough #fundraising time.
Not enough #marketing staff.
Not enough #brand budget.

So what’s the solution?

Focus the problem you solve, focus your programs, focus your outputs and outcomes, your vision, your donor targets, your messaging, and website and communications channels and grant applications and strategic plan.

The world needs you overflowing, not drained.

Focus, then watch the funding flow.

💪🏽💛

(Image by Pejman Milani)
posts.core_message: When resources are limited, sharply focus your problem, programs, messaging, donor targets, and communications so you conserve energy and increase the likelihood of funding.

posts.post_content:
Stop chasing the wrong type of donor.

It’s impossible to maintain them all.

Instead, get to know your five options in this #infographic. ⤵

Each category has its donor motivations, advantages and constraints for you, plus capabilities you need internally to attract and retain those funders. That’s why most big nonprofits focus their #fundraising on one or two types.

Click full screen to download the PDF.

Then repost ♻️ to help a leader in need.

Right donors, right fit.
Rocket fuel for your #brand.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Don’t try to chase every donor type—identify which of the five donor categories fit your organization and focus internal capacity on attracting and retaining one or two that will fuel your brand.

posts.post_content:
Nobody will read all that you write.

Not your donors nor your team.

Because humans scan headlines, browse subject lines, and skim text. Even when we actively choose to read something online, research shows we spend just 26 seconds looking at it. 

(Case in point: I’ve already lost some of you.)

That’s why lengthy text destroys your #fundraising game.

So get three #writing tips in this 1-minute guide.

Keep it short.
Keep it funded.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Because people scan online content, long copy damages fundraising results—write concisely to improve donor engagement and outcomes.

posts.post_content:
Fundraising is tough if you’re not tough.

So is nonprofit leadership.

But many of us struggle to develop thick skin. These five mental models from Ben Meer help you communicate boldly, process negative donor feedback, and constantly improve your brand.

Repost ♻️ to help your network.

Then remember two things:

“Mental toughness is believing I would prevail in my circumstances rather than believing my circumstances would change,” says LaRae Quy.

And...

“Resilience is very different than being numb,” reminds Yasmin Mogahed. “Resilience means you experience, you feel, you fail, you hurt. You fall. But, you keep going.”

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Developing mental toughness and resilience—by embracing feedback, feeling setbacks, and believing you will prevail—is essential for effective fundraising and nonprofit leadership.

posts.post_content:
Nonprofit teams are burned out.

#AI can help carry the load.

Because generative artificial intelligence allows you to create text, photos, video, audio, and other donor content in a few clicks. It’s like adding new communications staff overnight.

These 40 tools are capacity building, redefined.

So replace burnout with bandwidth.

Click full screen to download the PDF.
↳ Then repost ♻️ to help your network.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Generative AI tools can rapidly expand nonprofit communications capacity—acting like extra staff—and help replace team burnout with bandwidth.

posts.post_content:
Humility is killing your fundraising.

So boldly claim your results.

Of course, you often want to mention the partners involved in your work.  

And of course, “as small charities we have a culture of not shouting about our impact,” says Selena Blakeley. “But these small shifts could make all the difference.”

Here’s how in a 1-minute guide with three client examples. ⤵

Stop hiding the win.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Excessive humility and underplaying impact undermines fundraising—boldly claim and showcase your results, because small shifts in how you communicate wins can make a big difference.

posts.post_content:
𝗥𝗲𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁: donor presentations.
𝗘𝗺𝗯𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗲: fundraising conversations.

I’ve used this one mantra more in my own fundraising and sales career, than anything else. Because research found that collaborating with a prospect is the second key behavior to funding success, right after educating them.

Here’s how to reject one-way pitches:

🥇 𝗔𝘀𝗸 𝗹𝗼𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀.
The better your questions, the better your answers. Because you’ll know how to tailor your side of the chat.

🥈 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗸 𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝘆.
Show your deck only to spark the conversation, then take it off-screen and send it as a follow-up. I do this myself and watch people’s eyes fill with relief.

🥉 𝗔𝗶𝗺 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗸 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 ~𝟰𝟬% 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲.
Even if the prospect asks you to present, getting them to talk is better. Case in point: studies show the highest close rates at a 43/57 talk-to-listen ratio.

Like somebody’s wise mother once said:

We have two ears and one mouth for a reason. Let’s shut up and listen. (Especially us men.)

Seek a partnership.
Not a performance.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Stop treating donor meetings as one-way presentations and instead run collaborative fundraising conversations—ask lots of questions, use your pitch deck sparingly, and aim to speak only ~40% of the time—to increase funding success.

posts.post_content:
Nine months since USAID was gutted.

Leaders still gasping for support.

So use this infographic for your #fundraising. Because the top foundations sit on trillions in the bank. Enough to erase the USAID gap, overnight.

Repost ♻️ to help a leader in need.

Then don’t wait for donors.
Win them.

💪🏽💛

(𝘋𝘈𝘛𝘈 𝘚𝘖𝘜𝘙𝘊𝘌 — 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘳𝘪𝘥𝘨𝘦𝘴𝘱𝘢𝘯 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘶𝘱, 𝘏𝘪𝘨𝘩-𝘐𝘮𝘱𝘢𝘤𝘵 𝘗𝘩𝘪𝘭𝘢𝘯𝘵𝘩𝘳𝘰𝘱𝘺: 𝘎𝘪𝘷𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘉𝘦𝘵𝘵𝘦𝘳 𝘈𝘤𝘳𝘰𝘴𝘴 𝘈𝘴𝘪𝘢 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘞𝘰𝘳𝘭𝘥)
posts.core_message: Use this infographic to proactively target top foundations—who hold trillions and could replace lost USAID funding—and repost it to help leaders win donors rather than passively wait.

posts.post_content:
𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝘀𝗮𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴: We won a new grant!

𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝘀𝗮𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴: They won a new grantee.

𝗕𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹: We both won a new partnership.

Why?

“Nonprofits need the funds, but the donor also needs results and impact,” says Mickness Aeschlimann. “It’s a win-win situation for all.”

Another way to frame it. ⤵

“It’s not about money,” says Larry C Johnson. “It’s about two parties coming together to accomplish something they cannot do alone.”

But aren’t we supposed to make the donor the hero?

Rubbish.

I believe that’s the worst, most inequitable fundraising advice. Because even us nonprofits aren’t the heroes. We may use storytelling to invite the donor into the narrative too, but it’s our communities facing and fighting injustice that are heroic. 

So instead of fundraising with a 𝘩𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘰𝘶𝘵 for the donor’s money, communicate like it’s a 𝘩𝘢𝘯𝘥𝘴𝘩𝘢𝘬𝘦 between allies.

Your brand will be bolder. 
You’ll get more funder attention.
Conversations will become more just.

And we can shift the doer-donor power imbalance, once and for all.

You are the prize. 

Prizes don’t chase.
And stars don’t audition.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Stop framing fundraising as donors being heroes or nonprofits begging for money; instead frame wins as partnerships that center community heroes and communicate like a handshake between allies to rebalance power.

posts.post_content:
Communications isn’t overhead.

Marketing isn’t misuse.

​​Because “nonprofits are in the business of developing and advancing big, bold ideas,” says Sean Gibbons. “If you want your ideas to take hold and win, you need to communicate and communicate well.”

A grantmaker put it perfectly:

“Tell me one major, successful social change initiative that did not have very strong communications as part of its success.”

The hypocrisy?

This scorching TED Talk exposes the donor discrimination and double standard. 

So comms isn’t a line item.
It’s your lifeline.

Comms is not a cost.
It’s how change catches fire.

Do you agree?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Communications and marketing are not overhead or misuse but essential, catalytic work that enables nonprofit ideas to win—so funders who treat them as costs are hypocritical.

posts.post_content:
Your funding pitch should sound like:

1. The problem 
2. Our solution
3. The outcome

What most sound like:

1. Our solution
2. Our solution
3. Our solution
4. Our solution
5. Our solution

No shame.

But the opening and closing aren’t supposed to be about you. Only talk about your organization or mission in the middle.

Scroll for a simple formula you can remember. ⤵

“The pain is the pitch,” says Alex Hormozi.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Begin fundraising pitches with the problem, position your organization as the middle (the solution), and close with the outcome—don’t open or close by talking about yourself because 'the pain is the pitch.'

posts.post_content:
A big donor once admitted why they don’t invest.

The reason might surprise you.

This institutional funder told me the most common problem isn’t the idea, pitch deck, or #nonprofit leader. Instead, the number one reason they say 𝘯𝘰 is when the organization has no plan to pull off its vision.

In other words, no clear #strategy.

So your 2026 strategic plan can make or break your #fundraising. And make or break your team’s ability to turn a theory of change into impact.

Avoid these 7 mistakes. ⤵

Your plan is your funding lifeline.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors frequently say no not because of the idea or leader but because organizations have no clear plan to execute their vision—so a strong 2026 strategic plan is essential to secure funding and translate your theory of change into impact.

posts.post_content:
Your fundraising dreams won’t make a difference. 

Nor will your strategy.

Not without establishing internal routines to get it done. Because raising a lot of money requires two things:

📝 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗪𝗲 𝗪𝗶𝗻 (𝗦𝗧𝗥𝗔𝗧𝗘𝗚𝗬)
➕
👫🏽 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗪𝗲 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸 (𝗖𝗨𝗟𝗧𝗨𝗥𝗘)

“Vision without action is a daydream.”

And goals can guide, but routines realize funding.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Vision and strategy won’t raise significant funds by themselves—establishing routines and a culture of how you work is what turns goals into real fundraising results.

posts.post_content:
Bad fonts ruin a good fundraising message.

From websites to grant applications.

If your #messaging doesn't look good on the page, donors won’t read it. But research found that changing a single font increased reading speed and comprehension by 35%.

So you should learn basic #typography. 

Get this five-minute guide and don’t let the number of pages scare you — it’s quick and fun.

Polish your fonts.
Polish your #fundraising.

💪🏽💛

(𝘊𝘙𝘌𝘋𝘐𝘛: 𝘗𝘪𝘦𝘳𝘳𝘪𝘤𝘬 𝘊𝘢𝘭𝘷𝘦𝘻)
posts.core_message: Poor typography undermines fundraising because unreadable text drives donors away, so fundraisers should learn basic typography—quickly (five minutes)—to noticeably improve readability and fundraising results.

posts.post_content:
To all you rookie CEOs:

You’re not alone.

Most leaders have never done this before either. Not to mention, there’s no standard degree for becoming an Executive Director. Then donors and boards rarely provide you with a roadmap for leadership success.

It’s a hard, lonely gig.

So I analyzed the top leader profile of 200 of the sector’s best-funded, brightest nonprofits to see what patterns emerged.

Slide right to see 7 tough truths. ⤵

Nonprofit leaders are built, not born.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofit leadership is learned—not innate—so rookie CEOs shouldn’t feel alone, and studying top leaders reveals patterns that can guide them.

posts.post_content:
Donors don’t act because of your data.

Here’s the twist most people miss:

Neuroscience proves that even the most evidence-based funders first decide based on emotional signals, like your storytelling or leadership team, 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘯 justify their gift with logic.

(Even if they don’t admit it.)

“The brain processes meaning before detail,” says fundraising firm For Impact | The Suddes Group.

Get five tips in this 1-minute guide. ⤵

Like I tell our consulting clients:

Hearts open before minds do.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors decide to give based on emotional signals—like storytelling and visible leadership—and only afterwards use data to justify the gift, so fundraisers should lead with emotion first, then provide evidence.

posts.post_content:
You’re not raising money.

You’re raising feeling.

Because donors initially decide based on emotional signals, then justify that gift with logic, according to Harvard Business Review.

If your brand feels bland, your fundraising will too.

So take this 10-question quiz. There are a dozen brand characters to help your message captivate and compel donors:

🎨 𝗖𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿
👑 𝗥𝘂𝗹𝗲𝗿
🫱🏼‍🫲🏾 𝗖𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿
⚖️ 𝗖𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗻
🤡 𝗝𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿
💘 𝗟𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿
🦸🏻‍♀️ 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗼
🪄 𝗠𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗻
✊🏽 𝗥𝗲𝗯𝗲𝗹
🚀 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗿
🦉 𝗦𝗮𝗴𝗲
😇 𝗜𝗻𝗻𝗼𝗰𝗲𝗻𝘁

Which personality are you?

Show your soul, then show your strategy.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Effective fundraising depends on evoking emotion through a distinctive brand personality—show your organization's 'soul' (brand character) to captivate donors before you present strategy.

posts.post_content:
Face it: there are 10 million other nonprofits.

Millions of donors too.

So how do you determine the right fundraising fit? Just find the overlap of:

1️⃣ What you’re good at
2️⃣ What others aren’t doing
3️⃣ What funders will support
4️⃣ What the community needs

(Too often we forget the second part.)

That’s your brand sweet spot.
Your positioning perfection.
Your funding bullseye.

Focused message.
Funded mission.

💪🏽💛

P.S. This concept comes from my four-time bestselling book 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚: 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘥-𝘕𝘦𝘸 𝘞𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘰 𝘍𝘪𝘹 𝘠𝘰𝘶𝘳 𝘕𝘰𝘯𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘧𝘪𝘵 𝘍𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘴𝘪𝘯𝘨 → Get your copy at fundablefindable.org
posts.core_message: Find the overlap between what you do well, what others aren’t doing, what funders will support, and what the community needs — that 'brand sweet spot' is your positioning for sustained funding.

posts.post_content:
Not social media. Not events. Not public relations or paid advertising. Or speeches or podcasts or video. Not even direct mail. 

Only one fundraising tactic earns you $36 for every $1 spent:

Email marketing.

It’s your highest return on investment, by far, of any nonprofit communications channel. 

Why this matters. ⤵

You might be letting sexier tactics steal precious resources from what’s tried and true. Because four billion people use email every day: more than half of humanity, still growing, and roughly the same number as social media.

(Yet I’ve heard people say “email is dead” for decades. And I’m always surprised how many nonprofits still don’t even have a newsletter.)

So scroll right for a 2-minute guide with five steps.

Then boost your email to boost your fundraising.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Email marketing is the single most cost-effective fundraising channel for nonprofits—returning roughly $36 for every $1 spent—so organizations should stop prioritizing flashier tactics and instead invest in building and using email.

posts.post_content:
Marketing to every type of donor gets you:


None.

Because when you aim your #fundraising at every funder type, every giving level, every possible interest area, your message becomes so general that no one feels personally addressed.

So you must pick a primary donor category.
↳ See below for an #infographic to help.

Fewer targets. Stronger funding.

Choose your donor.
Fill your glass.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Trying to market to every possible donor makes your messaging generic and ineffective—pick a primary donor category to target so your fundraising is stronger.

posts.post_content:
Fundraising is a writing game.

Donors don’t fund the best ideas.
↳ They fund the best-explained ideas.

The reason why is obvious. ⤵

Funding applications = writing
Positioning strategy = writing
Theory of change = writing
Email marketing = writing
External comms = writing
Marketing plans = writing
Internal comms = writing
Strategic plans = writing
Video scripts = writing
Social media = writing
M&E reports = writing
Pitch decks = writing
Speeches = writing
Websites = writing
Blogging = writing
SEO = writing
PR = writing

But many leaders still get tangled up in writing about what they do.

(Especially considering the Global North’s unfair dominance of donor dollars — many Global South leaders are writing in English as a second or third language.)

So here are seven quick writing tips to help.

“Words have power. Words are power. Words could be your power.” — Mohammed Qahtani

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Fundraising success hinges less on having the best idea and more on explaining ideas clearly and persuasively through strong writing, so nonprofit leaders should prioritize writing skills across all fundraising and communications work.

posts.post_content:
8 formulas to fix your fundraising. ⤵

From mission to marketing.

To help you be:

Fundable.
Findable.
Funded.

💪🏽💛

#nonprofit #fundraising #infographic
posts.core_message: Eight practical formulas—from mission to marketing—will make your nonprofit fundable, findable, and funded.

posts.post_content:
I misspelled my first book signing.

In my own book title.

Mortifying.

A few people had stopped me, as we launched Fundable & Findable™ at Skoll. And despite having the fancy author pens and acting playful, I was honestly nervous, figuring out how the hell to properly sign a book.

(So yes, zoom in, there are two Ls in 𝘍𝘪𝘯𝘥𝘢𝘣𝘭𝘦) 🙈

Why tell you this embarrassing story?

Because I often meet nonprofit CEOs who are afraid to get out there: scared to speak, write, be a thought leader until they’re “ready.” Yet, when building your personal brand, remember that nobody has it figured out.

None of us is ever ready.

“There are no adults,” says the legendary investor and thinker Naval Ravikant. “Everyone’s making it up as they go along.”

So speak. Write. Join that panel. Misspell a few words.

Fundraising favors the fearless.
Not the flawless.

💪🏽💛

P.S. What’s your worst ever typo?
posts.core_message: Don't wait until you're perfect—put yourself out there and be visible, because fundraising rewards boldness and action more than flawless execution.

posts.post_content:
Fundraising punishes perfectionism.

And rewards leaders who keep moving.

So here are eight enduring lessons from Janis Ozolins. I’m personally working on number three this year: the vulnerability to ask for help.

Which illustration is your favorite?

Focus, adjust, get funded.

💪🏽💛

#nonprofit #fundraising #leadership
posts.core_message: Fundraising favors leaders who keep making progress and embrace vulnerability rather than waiting for perfection, so adopt actionable habits (like asking for help) to get funded.

posts.post_content:
“Nike has said ‘Just Do It’ every single day since 1988 and you’re afraid to reshare an old blog post from 6 months ago because people may have seen it,” says Ross Simmonds.

With that reframe in mind, fundraising content doesn’t have to be so hard.

You might have enough already.

Just repurpose your existing communications materials into different formats. For example, take one impact report and break it up into:

↳ A 𝗰𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝘂𝗱𝘆 for donors
↳ A 𝘄𝗲𝗯𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗿 for NGO partners 
↳ A 𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗼 𝘁𝗼𝘂𝗿 for website visitors
↳ A 𝗵𝗼𝘄-𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗲 for other nonprofits
↳ A 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝗹𝗶𝗱𝗲𝘀 deck for media
↳ A 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲 for government officials
↳ A series of 𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗮𝗹𝘀 for the community

See which content types engage your donors.

37 formats, endless possibilities.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: You likely already have plenty of content—simplify fundraising by repurposing existing communications (e.g., an impact report) into multiple audience-tailored formats to increase engagement.

posts.post_content:
Death. Heights. Spiders.

No, the number 1 fear in the world?

Public speaking.

But there’s a simple way for you to improve, just like I did:

Write a lot and only talk about things you’ve typed. “Hardly any sentence in public comes out of my mouth, unless I’ve written it down once before,” says Neil deGrasse Tyson.

Why?

Your brain can only crystallize a unique point of view — and gain confidence in that perspective — when you’re forced to document it.

That’s why our MIGHTY ALLY clients who are outstanding 𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙗𝙖𝙡 communicators first find clarity through 𝙣𝙤𝙣-𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙗𝙖𝙡 communication.

Here’s my advice. ⤵

Before trying to be a conference panelist, join podcasts, or speak on any stage, you should regularly blog, post on social, or publish an email newsletter externally. Or even send a monthly staff memo internally.

My own keynotes (like this video from the India Fundraising Conference) are much stronger after writing every day for three years.

Sounds obvious, right?

But it’s all too common for leaders to try to become a speaker before putting in the time to be a writer.

So pen your thoughts, then voice your impact.

From keyboard to keynote.

💪🏽💛

P.S. Do you fear spiders or speaking more?
posts.core_message: If you want to be a confident, compelling public speaker, first develop your ideas by writing regularly—blogging, newsletters, or posts—which crystallizes your point of view and makes your spoken remarks far stronger.

posts.post_content:
You don’t need to sound 𝘴𝘮𝘢𝘳𝘵, much less 𝘣𝘳𝘪𝘭𝘭𝘪𝘢𝘯𝘵.

Even Albert Einstein believed that 𝘴𝘪𝘮𝘱𝘭𝘦 is the ultimate form of intelligence. So scroll right for nine ways to simplify your fundraising. ⤵

Make no mistake:

We’re not talking about dumbing down or ignoring the nuance of your work itself. What I’m saying is that complex problems and complex solutions don’t require complex communications.

Cut the fluff.
Keep the core.
Win the funding.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Simplify your fundraising communications—cut the fluff and keep the core—because complex problems and solutions don’t require complex messaging to win funding.

posts.post_content:
The number one complaint we hear from donors:

They don’t understand what you do.

Case in point. ⤵

Kevin Starr and Mulago Foundation are nonprofit masterminds. They’ve funded and fueled impactful organizations like Food for Education, One Acre Fund, Last Mile Health, and Educate Girls.

But his first priority for nonprofits isn’t scale.
Or monitoring and evaluation.
Or fundraising.

It’s communications.

Get clear.
Then get funded.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Clear, simple communications should be nonprofits' top priority because donors often don't understand what organizations do, and clarity is what unlocks funding.

posts.post_content:
The “convincing” mentality is dead.

Persuading doesn’t work.

Trust me, I used to push, push, push in my early sales and fundraising meetings. And a board advisor told me, “Kevin, prospects can smell desperation.”

In fact, research found that collaborating with a donor is our second most important behavior in winning a pitch, after clearly educating them.

So stop being a Convincer.
↳ Brian Gawor, CFRE says to be these other 4Cs:

1️⃣ 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗼𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿
Listen more. Collaborate deeper. Be their partner, not their salesperson.

2️⃣ 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁
Know them like a strategic advisor. Understand their tools, guide their giving.

3️⃣ 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿
Make giving social. Connect donors and grow your impact through networks.

4️⃣ 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗿𝗴𝗲
Don’t hide your mission’s experts. Introduce them for stronger engagement.

Dialogue beats monologue.

The harder you push, the more donors resist.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: The old 'convincing' approach no longer works in fundraising—instead prioritize dialogue and adopt collaborative roles (Collaborator, Consultant, Connector, Concierge) to educate, engage, and partner with donors.

posts.post_content:
Most donors give once and ghost you. And the reason why isn’t a secret:

❌ They weren’t thanked.
❌ They weren’t told how the gift was used.

Ouch.

That’s just basic communications. So here’s a simple #storytelling formula to get the donation and keep them coming back for more.

Right story.
At the right time.
Over and over again.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors often don't give again because you fail to thank them and show how their gift was used, so consistently telling the right story at the right time will turn one-off donors into repeat supporters.

posts.post_content:
Most of you ignore this #fundraising tactic.

Which is wild, given how well it works.

Your proven process.

If you can’t show donors the 𝘩𝘰𝘸, the 𝘸𝘩𝘺 and the 𝘸𝘩𝘢𝘵 don’t even matter. So learn to raise more cash with less chaos in this quick guide, including #nonprofit examples (one just got the $2 million Skoll Award).

“A strong process drives strong outcomes,” says Sasha Fisher.

And a strong process beats a strong pitch.

Every time.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: A clear, proven process that shows donors how you’ll achieve impact matters more than the pitch or the why/what and consistently produces stronger fundraising outcomes.

posts.post_content:
The fundraising myth: more is better.

That’s the lie we’ve all been sold.

But here’s the truth: most of your marketing is just noise. Endless chatter in an empty room, yet we mistake motion for momentum.

What’s the fix?

The minimalist communications playbook:

✔️ Monthly email newsletter
✔️ 3-line elevator pitch
✔️ 5-page website
✔️ 10-slide deck

You can do all four for cheap, or even for free. And, combined, I see our clients raise millions in funding.

Let’s be honest.

Your comms team is tiny. Stretched thin. Or nonexistent. So until those four primary marketing assets are 100% optimized, ignore everything else.

Not until you do the basic stuff well.

“You are everywhere, but you don’t have to be. Strategy is a decision to take a path, to say no,” says Kristina Halvorson.

Brands that chase all channels, chase away funding.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Focus on optimizing four core communications assets—a monthly newsletter, a 3-line elevator pitch, a 5‑page website, and a 10‑slide deck—because doing the basics well will generate more funding than spreading effort across every channel.

posts.post_content:
Donors buy into your vision, not just your cause.

But 6 mistakes hurt your #fundraising.

So slide right for this guide with pro tips.

Then, turn your vision into a funding force.
↳ And repost ♻️ to help your network.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Fundraising wins when you sell a compelling vision rather than just a cause—avoid six common mistakes and use these pro tips to turn your vision into funding.

posts.post_content:
The wrong fundraising advice:

Find a lane and stick to it.

The better advice:

Find the intersection of 2-3 lanes. Like these example niches. ⤵

🥉 Education (𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱)
🥈 Education + girls (𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿)
🥇 Education + girls + secondary (𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁)

🥉 Mental health (𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱)
🥈 Mental health + refugees (𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿)
🥇 Mental health + refugees + children (𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁)

🥉 Smallholder farms (𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱)
🥈 Smallholder farms + India (𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿)
🥇 Smallholder farms + India + greenhouses (𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁)

That’s how you move from 𝘜𝘯𝘥𝘪𝘧𝘧𝘦𝘳𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘢𝘵𝘦𝘥 to 𝘐𝘥𝘦𝘢𝘭 positioning. And that’s your unique edge in the donor’s brain.

Be known for something.
Or be remembered for nothing.

Your riches are in the niches.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Instead of sticking to a single broad lane, specialize by combining 2–3 specific niches to create differentiated, memorable positioning that gives you a unique edge with donors.

posts.post_content:
Say less.

Raise more.

Because good communication (and fundraising) is saying the most stuff in the fewest words.

That’s why I can’t stop referencing the book 𝘚𝘮𝘢𝘳𝘵 𝘉𝘳𝘦𝘷𝘪𝘵𝘺 since reading it. Now, you can get its top tips for your messaging.

Scroll right for this 2-minute read.
↳ Then repost ♻️ to help your network.

Fewer words, more donations.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Brevity in messaging—saying the most in the fewest words—makes fundraising more effective, so apply Smart Brevity principles to raise more.

posts.post_content:
Most nonprofit CEOs feel unseen. Unheard.

But visibility isn’t your first priority.

And most thought leaders want a platform, but platforms come from proof.

So if you’re struggling to use your personal brand to help with fundraising, if social posts aren’t landing, if no one’s inviting you to speak, or if your advocacy has stalled… maybe it’s not about your content.

Maybe it’s about credibility.

Like a famous comedian once told rookie comics trying to break through the noise, “be so good they can’t ignore you.”

That applies to nonprofit thought leadership too:

✔️ Build an organization that delivers results
✔️ Drive change others want to learn from
✔️ Make people ask, “How did they pull that off?”

When you do, visibility becomes inevitable.

We grew MIGHTY ALLY for six years before I started here on LinkedIn.

And our client Community Health Impact Coalition built a movement first, then we built the new CHIC brand. Finally, Dr. Madeleine Ballard built upon her own thought leadership. A $2 million Skoll Award followed.

Prove it. Then promote it.

You got this.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Prioritize building real organizational results and credibility first—prove your impact—because visibility, platform, and fundraising will follow naturally.

posts.post_content:
I failed on Twitter.

And learned a hard lesson you might avoid.

I learned the same lesson by failing on Instagram too, with only 512 followers. But it doesn’t matter; I’m at 99,000 followers on #LinkedIn and my newsletter (just launched last year) already has 14,000 subscribers:

kevinlbrown.substack.com

So here’s the takeaway for you thought leaders. ⤵

Experiment with a few #communications channels, watch the data, but fail fast. Then figure out your best couple of platforms, and ignore everything else. Don’t be swayed by how other thought leaders do it.

They might have stumbled where you shine.
They might be stretched too thin across media.
And they might play a different #fundraising game.

Yes, I sometimes envy those with a big Instagram or X following or YouTube channel. But two main platforms were plenty to launch my bestselling book Fundable & Findable™.

Remain focused.

Be known somewhere.
Not forgotten everywhere.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Experiment across platforms, fail fast, then concentrate on the one or two channels where you perform best instead of spreading yourself thin trying to be everywhere.

posts.post_content:
“Want more funding for your mission?

Read this book.”

Of all the 5-star reviews of 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚, this one blooms.

Because it comes from the fundraising expert and philanthropist, Natalie Rekstad. And it endorses the root-level premise of my book: 

Brand is the soil; fundraising is the water.
Brand is the engine; fundraising is the fuel.
Brand is aspiration; fundraising is realization.
Brand is the foundation; fundraising is the roof.

In other words, both brand and fundraising are critical. And inseparable. It’s not about the order of 𝘪𝘮𝘱𝘰𝘳𝘵𝘢𝘯𝘤𝘦, it’s about the order of 𝘰𝘱𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘴.

To get funding, be fundable and findable.

First.

Get your copy.
↳ fundablefindable.org

💪🏽💛

P.S. Please repost ♻️ to help our ongoing book launch!
posts.core_message: Brand and fundraising are inseparable but serve different operational roles — make your organization fundable and findable first so you can actually get funding.

posts.post_content:
To be a leader, lead.
To be a CEO, execute.
To be a speaker, speak.
To be an artist, create art.
To be an activist, activate.
To be a mobilizer, mobilize.
To be an innovator, innovate.
To be a storyteller, tell stories.
To be an influencer, influence.
To be a visionary, cast a vision.
To be a brand, build your brand.

And to be funded, be fundable first.

Recognition doesn’t come from claiming a title. It comes from action, often for a long time before anyone notices.

And the same goes for your #fundraising.

“Let go of the thing that you’re trying to be (the noun), and focus on the actual work you need to be doing (the verb),” says Austin Kleon.

My wedding vows included a similar sentiment — from a cheesy country music song — “love isn’t something that we’re in, it’s something that we do.” But it took me years to appreciate this #leadership advice at work.

Actions speak.
Titles whisper.

Do the verb you wish to be.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Recognition and funding follow sustained action—focus on doing the work (the verbs), not merely claiming titles (the nouns).

posts.post_content:
A NEW communications cheat sheet. 👇🏽

Used by our 450 clients in 60 countries.

But your marcom plan isn’t just for funding.

“Nonprofits are in the business of developing and advancing big, bold ideas,” says Sean Gibbons. “If you want your ideas to take hold and win, you need to communicate and communicate well. It’s not an option anymore — it’s a necessity.”

Click full screen to download the PDF.

Then ♻️ repost for a leader who might need it.

It’s time to grab the megaphone.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Strong nonprofit communications are essential for advancing big ideas—not just for fundraising—so download and use this proven cheat sheet to communicate better and amplify your work.

posts.post_content:
Hate fundraising? Funding will hate you back.

Loathe raising money? Money will fail you.

So here’s some straight talk. ⤵

“Most CEOs would rather have a root canal than make a solicitation call on a wealthy prospect,” says Nonprofit World. But “if you’re not willing to raise money, you shouldn’t be in the CEO position. It’s part and parcel of the job.”

YOU own the brand.
YOU are best at storytelling.
YOU are the number one fundraiser.

“Fear of fundraising = fear of leading by example,” says Judy Reyes-Henderson.

After working with 450 growth-stage nonprofits in 60 countries — and running a nonprofit hybrid myself — this truth holds without exception.

Yes, it may only be 50% of your time, focused on high-value donors. And yes, development staff, comms teams, and consultants should help you.

But you’re the cash catalyst.
The fundraising flame.
The gold getter.

You got this.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Nonprofit CEOs must personally own and lead fundraising—using their brand and storytelling to solicit high-value donors—even if it’s uncomfortable, because effective leadership requires it.

posts.post_content:
You have 3 seconds.

Lose them, lose the gift.

So here are the two most critical rules in your #communications:

1️⃣ 𝗦𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗶𝘁𝘆
2️⃣ 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆

The average adult reads at a 12-year-old level and it takes seven impressions for people to remember your brand. If you use plain language then repeatedly deliver that same #fundraising message, donors stick around.

And you earn the 𝘺𝘦𝘴.

“Genius can be recognized by its childish simplicity,” says the Chinese proverb.

Be simple.
Be consistent.
Be funded.

💪🏽💛

P.S. “A confused donor always says no” is a line from my four-time bestselling book 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚: 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘥-𝘕𝘦𝘸 𝘞𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘰 𝘍𝘪𝘹 𝘠𝘰𝘶𝘳 𝘕𝘰𝘯𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘧𝘪𝘵 𝘍𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘴𝘪𝘯𝘨 → Get your copy at fundablefindable.org
posts.core_message: Use plain-language messaging (simplicity) and deliver it repeatedly (consistency) so donors understand, remember your brand, and are more likely to give.

posts.post_content:
Michael Jordan missed 12,345 shots in his NBA career.

So if you have failed at fundraising:

Good.
I have too.
That’s the point.
Now go fail a lot more.

Because there’s a key for every lock and a donor for every nonprofit, so you being 𝘤𝘰𝘯𝘴𝘪𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘭𝘺 𝘢𝘷𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘨𝘦 will raise more money than if you’re 𝘪𝘯𝘤𝘰𝘯𝘴𝘪𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘭𝘺 𝘣𝘳𝘪𝘭𝘭𝘪𝘢𝘯𝘵.

We need your fundraising resilience. It’s a marathon, not a sprint. And consistency wins the funding race.

“You miss 100% of the shots you don’t take.” — Wayne Gretzky

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Embrace failure and prioritize consistent, persistent fundraising—because regular, 'average' efforts beat intermittent brilliance and resilience wins the long-term funding race.

posts.post_content:
Your impact data isn’t the fundraising problem.

The issue is how to use it.

Because:

1️⃣ Everyone else is claiming ‘evidence-based’ these days.

2️⃣ You can’t raise money with a randomized controlled trial alone.

3️⃣ A monitoring & evaluation report (itself) doesn’t land you funding.

Remember. ⤵

“Data are just summaries of thousands of stories; tell a few of those stories to help make the data meaningful,” says Made to Stick.

And science tells us a fact wrapped in a story is 22 times more memorable.

So don’t just count numbers.
Make the numbers count.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Impact data alone won’t raise funds — you must wrap data in a few compelling stories to make the numbers memorable and persuasive.

posts.post_content:
Donors don’t fund ideas.

They fund 𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘥𝘦𝘳𝘴 with ideas.

So your leadership style is either a #fundraising catalyst… or a problem. After working with more than 450 organizations in 60 countries (and being a nonprofit leader myself) we’ve spotted a pattern.

There are 7 effective #leadership styles:

🏋🏽 The Doer
🧐 The Decider
🎬 The Director
💡 The Dreamer
🥁 The Drummer
👩🏻‍🤝‍👨🏿 The Delegator
🗣️ The Discusser

Plus one to avoid:

🙅🏾 The Deflector

Which one(s) are you?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors invest in leaders, not ideas, so your leadership style determines whether you attract funding—adopt one of seven effective styles and avoid the 'Deflector'.

posts.post_content:
Fundraising is a dance.

But your writing lacks rhythm.

And donors want stories that move them, but your #messaging echoes in empty halls.

So embrace the music in writing, make your sentences swing, and write not just with ink, but with melody, like this #infographic.

Let each word sing.

Then funding will harmonize.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Treat fundraising writing like music—use rhythmic, melodic storytelling that moves donors so funding will follow.

posts.post_content:
Bold messaging ignites bold fundraising.

But most of you aren’t writers.

So use this infographic with 130 emotion-triggering words for your copywriting. Start in the center and move out to level up your language.

A couple of examples. 👇🏽

𝗕𝗘𝗙𝗢𝗥𝗘
We’re 𝘴𝘢𝘥 and 𝘴𝘶𝘳𝘱𝘳𝘪𝘴𝘦𝘥 at the government’s policy change.

𝗔𝗙𝗧𝗘𝗥
We’re 𝘦𝘮𝘱𝘵𝘺 and 𝘥𝘪𝘴𝘮𝘢𝘺𝘦𝘥 at the government’s 𝘥𝘪𝘴𝘮𝘪𝘴𝘴𝘪𝘷𝘦 policy change.


𝗕𝗘𝗙𝗢𝗥𝗘
The poverty is 𝘣𝘢𝘥 in our community, so we’re 𝘩𝘢𝘱𝘱𝘺 to receive this funding.

𝗔𝗙𝗧𝗘𝗥
The poverty is 𝘰𝘶𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘤𝘰𝘯𝘵𝘳𝘰𝘭 in our community, so we’re 𝘪𝘯𝘴𝘱𝘪𝘳𝘦𝘥 by this 𝘤𝘰𝘶𝘳𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘰𝘶𝘴 funding.


I even used it after the first draft of this post.

𝗕𝗘𝗙𝗢𝗥𝗘
Bold messaging 𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘥𝘴 𝘵𝘰 bold fundraising.

𝗔𝗙𝗧𝗘𝗥
Bold messaging 𝘪𝘨𝘯𝘪𝘵𝘦𝘴 bold fundraising.


See how easy?

Click full screen to download. 

Repost ♻️ to help your network.

Then go forth and phrase powerfully.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Replace bland words with emotion-triggering language—using the shared 130-word infographic—to make your fundraising messages bolder and more effective.

posts.post_content:
Get your team back in line after the holidays, before you need a traffic cop to do it for you.

Here’s how. ⤵

In your 2026 annual planning offsite or next team meeting, just be sure to remind your staff of six key things:

1️⃣ Why you exist (reason)
2️⃣ What you do (mission)
3️⃣ Where you’re going (vision)
4️⃣ Who will do it (team)
5️⃣ How to get there (rhythms)
6️⃣ When it will be done (priorities)

That’s your theory of change and strategic plan. That’s how you align an organization. And that’s what it takes to 𝗯𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲.

Don’t have a (good) theory of change or strategic plan? My bestselling book 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚: 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘥-𝘕𝘦𝘸 𝘞𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘰 𝘍𝘪𝘹 𝘠𝘰𝘶𝘳 𝘕𝘰𝘯𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘧𝘪𝘵 𝘍𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘴𝘪𝘯𝘨 has six full chapters on those topics, with action steps, illustrations, and blueprints.

Fundable teams move in sync.
Unity is your fundraising strategy.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: In your next planning session, explicitly set six elements—why you exist, what you do, where you’re going, who will do it, how you’ll get there, and when it will be done—to align your team, clarify your theory of change, and become fundable.

posts.post_content:
The wrong way to begin your 2026 strategy:

❌ We plan to accomplish...
❌ I hope to achieve...
❌ Our goals are...

The right way:

✔️ Partners want...
✔️ Participants need...
✔️ Funders are saying...

Big difference.

“We spend a lot of time designing the bridge, but not enough time thinking about the people who are crossing it,” says Prabhjot Singh, MD PhD. 

So just like I lead our strategy workshops, see if you can break the habit of thinking and talking about your organization first.

And remember: 

“There’s a tendency among entrepreneurs to focus inward, on our needs, desires, goals, hopes, and dreams,” says Tiny Little Business. “Everything changes when we shift our focus to the needs and desires of the audience we serve.”

Make it about them, not us.

Listen, then act.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: When you build next year’s strategy, start by centering the needs and signals of partners, participants, and funders—not your organization’s internal goals.

posts.post_content:
Local leaders don’t need foreign saviors.

Or aid parachutes. 

“For trust-based philanthropy to really shift power we must then extend it beyond grantees to trusting communities to drive their own change processes in their entirety,” says Nina Blackwell.

Parachute philanthropy wants the photo.
Local leaders want the fix.

Parachute philanthropy seeks credit.
Local leaders seek change.

Parachute philanthropy sells stories.
Local leaders live them.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors should stop 'parachute philanthropy' that prioritizes photos and credit, and instead trust and empower local leaders and communities to drive and own meaningful change.

posts.post_content:
Don’t tell donors how awesome you are.

It doesn’t work well.

Because we all say we’re awesome. Instead, you can use a subtle (but smart!) tactic:

Social proof.

“Even someone inclined to support your cause may not give unless you push the right buttons,” says Claire Axelrad. “Of all the ways to do that, social proof is among the easiest and most successful.”

“When you say your organization rocks, who really believes you?”

“When someone else says the same thing, that’s another ballgame entirely. Their independent seal of approval acts as a decision-making shortcut, helping resolve any doubts your organization is top notch and worthy of investment.”

So how can you use social proof in your brand?

1️⃣ Donor logos on all your materials

2️⃣ Case studies to show instead of tell

3️⃣ Awards/recognitions you’ve received

4️⃣ Participant and customer impact stories

5️⃣ Reviews like GuideStar or Charity Navigator

6️⃣ Name dropping when speaking with funders

7️⃣ Fundraising events hosted by existing donors

8️⃣ Articles and media mentions about your work

9️⃣ Quotes and testimonials singing your praises
↳ Just like I used on this post


Instead of pitching you on my book 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚: 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘥-𝘕𝘦𝘸 𝘞𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘰 𝘍𝘪𝘹 𝘠𝘰𝘶𝘳 𝘕𝘰𝘯𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘧𝘪𝘵 𝘍𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘴𝘪𝘯𝘨, these testimonials say it all. 

That’s social proof.

I proved my point.
Now prove yours.

Social proof worked here.
Make it work for you.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Stop telling donors how great you are and instead deploy third‑party social proof (testimonials, logos, awards, media mentions, reviews) to resolve doubts and increase giving.

posts.post_content:
Everyone tells you to use storytelling.

Like it’s a fundraising magic wand. 🪄

But how?

The best storytelling formula I use personally is also the easiest to remember. Because the book 𝘔𝘢𝘥𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘚𝘵𝘪𝘤𝘬 distills 10 years of research into six simple steps for your next campaign, speech, or funding pitch.

Learn the 𝗦𝗨𝗖𝗖𝗘𝗦𝘀 formula in this infographic.

Unforgettable stories =
unstoppable funding.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Use the SUCCES formula from Made to Stick—a six-step, easy-to-remember storytelling framework—to craft unforgettable fundraising campaigns, speeches, and pitches that drive funding.

posts.post_content:
I thought recording my own audiobook would be simple.

But the studio heard every mistake.

Every breath, every doubt.

It was a crash course in vulnerability, but now (only six months after the audiobook release) we’ve already sold 600 copies of 𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 & 𝙁𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚: 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘥-𝘕𝘦𝘸 𝘞𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘰 𝘍𝘪𝘹 𝘠𝘰𝘶𝘳 𝘕𝘰𝘯𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘧𝘪𝘵 𝘍𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘳𝘢𝘪𝘴𝘪𝘯𝘨.

And we’re approaching 5,000 total copies for the first year!

So tune in on Audible, Apple, Spotify, Google Play, and more. Including a Listen Online version that works on any device, anywhere (plus supports our own nonprofit mission the most).

fundablefindable.org

Give it a listen.
Turn the page.
Get funded.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Recording my audiobook forced me into vulnerability, and that honesty has driven measurable success—so tune in on major platforms to listen and support our nonprofit mission.

posts.post_content:
