You thanked the donor, but did they thank 𝙮𝙤𝙪?

They should.

Because they got your impact, just like you got their donation. It’s a win/win deal.

“At the deepest level, there is no giver, no gift, and no recipient... only the universe rearranging itself,” says Jon Kabat-Zinn.

So rethink your fundraising communications.
Less savior, more solidarity.

Handshakes, not handouts.
Allies, not applicants.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Fundraising communications should treat donations as a mutual exchange—thank donors in ways that emphasize solidarity and partnership rather than saviorism.

posts.post_content:
“What the hell does donor stewardship even mean?”

↳ I asked on a recent podcast.

My question was blunt, because “stewardship” isn’t working. Just look at our sector’s dismal donor retention rates:

💷 Nearly 7 out of 10 donors will give once and never again.

💴 Every $100 gained is offset by $96 in losses through donor attrition.

💵 Last year, donor counts dropped 7.6% and donations decreased 1.3% (the third consecutive year to decline).


Let’s consider the main reasons donors don’t give again:

❌ They weren’t told how their first gift was used.
❌ They didn’t feel thanked.

That’s just basic communications.

And that’s why author Kay Grace says the real work begins 𝘢𝘧𝘵𝘦𝘳 you receive the gift. “This is the most important, yet most neglected piece of the fundraising process.”

So remember the rule of 7. ⤵

For each funding request, balance it with seven engaging brand touchpoints, like:

→ Email surveys
→ Program tours
→ Video updates
→ Thank-you calls
→ Thank-you notes
→ Email newsletters
→ In-person meetings
→ Social media tagging
→ Milestone celebrations
→ Asking for advice/input
→ Annual report mentions
→ Behind-the-scenes peeks
→ Text & WhatsApp messages
→ Gift confirmation & tax receipt
→ Invitations to events or webinars
→ Handwritten holiday or birthday cards 
→ Impact update on how the gift was used

Count them seven times in your communications plan.

Then count on them to give again.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donor stewardship is being neglected—pair every funding request with seven varied, meaningful touchpoints (thank-yous, impact updates, engagement opportunities) so donors know how their gift was used and will give again.

posts.post_content:
Your fundraising doesn’t need more words.

It needs sharper ones.

Because if you need a novel to persuade a donor, you’ve already lost.

Hook first.
Details come later.

“People are tempted to tell you everything, with perfect accuracy, right up front, when they should be giving you just enough info to be useful, then a little more, then a little more,” says the book 𝘔𝘢𝘥𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘚𝘵𝘪𝘤𝘬.

If it rambles, it repels.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Concise, sharply chosen copy that hooks donors immediately is more effective than long, overly detailed appeals — give just enough information and add more gradually.

posts.post_content:
I used to pitch for funding the wrong way.

And it failed.

I would prep, prep, prep like a stage performance and talk, talk, talk like it was my job to impress.

Here’s the problem. ⤵

“Asking for a contribution makes most people nervous, which causes rambling,” says Lori L. Jacobwith. “And together, we have the perfect storm to make the most common fundraising mistake: talking too much.”

“If you want to get a YES — use less words.”

Learn how with these 10 quick tips.

Reject presentations.
Embrace conversations.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Stop treating fundraising asks like a performance—use far fewer words and turn presentations into conversations to increase the chance of getting a “yes.”

posts.post_content:
Don’t yell your fundraising appeals.

Stronger and louder isn’t always better.

Here’s a quick analogy. ⤵

Whistling can travel up to 10,000 meters. But shouting carries less than 180. It’s the same logic as a laser vs. torch. Or chisel vs. hammer. 

The lesson for your fundraising?

Focus your communications.
Focus your messaging.
Focus your channels.
Focus your teams.
Focus your work.

How?

Say NO more than you say YES in your theory of change, positioning strategy, and strategic plan. Be ruthless about what you 𝘥𝘰𝘯'𝘵 do and 𝘥𝘰𝘯'𝘵 say. 

The tighter the brand, the bigger the bucks.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Focused, selective communications and a tight brand produce better fundraising results than loud, unfocused appeals.

posts.post_content:
Your team should be mocking you.

Because you repeat the same message.

Over and over and over again.

Why?

Regular internal communication is different from micromanagement. That’s telling people 𝘩𝘰𝘸 to do their jobs.

Overcommunication is constantly reminding your team 𝘸𝘩𝘺 their work matters, 𝘸𝘩𝘰 is responsible, 𝘸𝘩𝘢𝘵 they should be doing, 𝘸𝘩𝘦𝘯 it needs to be done, and 𝘸𝘩𝘦𝘳𝘦 the organization is going in the future.

That’s just good #leadership.

And that leads to maximized funding, because you operate more efficiently and make the most of your #fundraising.

So repeat, repeat, repeat your messaging, theory of change, and #strategy. Repeat your brand.

Until it sticks.

And “over-communicate,” says Alex Irvine. “It’s better to tell someone something they already know than to not tell them something they needed to hear.”

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Leaders should deliberately and repeatedly over-communicate mission, roles, priorities, and strategy so teams are aligned, operate more efficiently, and your fundraising is maximized.

posts.post_content:
We’ve abused the word 𝙨𝙘𝙖𝙡𝙚.

And worn out the term 𝙨𝙮𝙨𝙩𝙚𝙢𝙨 𝙘𝙝𝙖𝙣𝙜𝙚.

As a social sector, we’re notorious for hyping these two buzzwords. Then leaders feel pressured by funding to contort into something they’re not.

(Not to mention, those who 𝘢𝘳𝘦 scaling and changing systems get lost in the twisted jargon.)

There are actually four impact pathways. Thus, four messaging options for your fundraising:

1️⃣ Size
2️⃣ Scale
3️⃣ Sustainability
4️⃣ Systems change

Read more in this 1-minute guide.

Then explore every pathway, navigate your own brand, and silence the buzzword babble.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Stop misusing 'scale' and 'systems change'—recognize four distinct impact pathways (size, scale, sustainability, systems change) and use them as tailored fundraising messaging instead of succumbing to buzzword pressure.

posts.post_content:
“No one likes their time wasted,” says investor Morgan Housel. “It’s a universal annoyance. Make your point and get out of the way.”

The lesson from this Tom Fishburne cartoon?

Donors don’t want 𝘮𝘰𝘳𝘦 words, or 𝘭𝘰𝘯𝘨𝘦𝘳 fundraising #communications.

So if you got a new ChatGPT subscription for Christmas to help you write more, that’s backwards. Because #AI content is garbage when you start from scratch; AI’s strength is refining and condensing your existing message.

Your donors fund purpose, not paragraphs.

Value, not volume.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Donors prefer concise, purpose-driven communications, so use AI to refine and condense existing messages rather than to generate more or longer fundraising copy.

posts.post_content:
Don’t delegate all your messaging.

Or outsource all your #fundraising applications.

Because you don’t gain clarity first, then have someone write for you. Instead, clarity comes 𝘧𝘳𝘰𝘮 you writing.

So sure, you can hire a copywriter or use AI for extra sizzle, get your comms team to add the final creative polish, or partner with a fundraising firm to repurpose donor appeals. 

You can’t, however, pay a writer to think for you.

Case in point:

Our best brand work at MIGHTY ALLY comes with clients who already publish a lot of content. Then we extract vs. dictate truths. Focus vs. fabricate. And weave together a messaging platform vs. build a narrative from scratch.

That’s why you should at least get a draft started and write some sort of #content.

✔️ Books
✔️ Op-eds
✔️ Research
✔️ Blog posts
✔️ Whitepapers
✔️ Social media
✔️ Case studies
✔️ Press releases
✔️ Annual reports
✔️ Email newsletters

… any #marketing that involves putting thoughts into words.

“When we write, we have to persuade others,” says Sara Booth. “And this means we have to define how we got from Point A to Point N. And at that point we often find out that we don’t know.”

Writing transforms fleeting thoughts into lasting wisdom.

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Clarity in your nonprofit's messaging comes from doing the writing yourself—draft your ideas to discover and refine them, and then use copywriters, AI, or agencies only to polish and package that thinking.

posts.post_content:
You should 𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙣 𝙤𝙛𝙛 a lot of donors.

That way, you 𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙣 𝙤𝙣 others.

Because a test of fearless fundraising is this: some donors should love your brand, but many should not.

So if nobody disagrees with your position, go bolder.

“Contrarian thinking that polarizes opinion is vital for true thought leadership that can drive change,” says Sarika Goel.

The best art divides the audience.

“Thousands of people don’t like what I do,” says bestselling author James Patterson. “Fortunately, millions do.”

Aim to be a thought leader who shakes things up in your fundraising, questions the status quo, and inspires others to do the same.

Donors fund the bold, not the bland. 

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Be deliberately bold and polarizing in your fundraising—it's okay to alienate some donors because that polarization attracts passionate supporters and drives change.

posts.post_content:
“This book isn’t just theory...

It’s a survival guide.”

Powerful words from Enock Nsubuga, in his 𝘉𝘰𝘰𝘬 𝘙𝘦𝘷𝘪𝘦𝘸: 𝘍𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘢𝘣𝘭𝘦 & 𝘍𝘪𝘯𝘥𝘢𝘣𝘭𝘦 – 𝘈 𝘉𝘭𝘶𝘦𝘱𝘳𝘪𝘯𝘵 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘕𝘰𝘯𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘧𝘪𝘵𝘴 & 𝘚𝘰𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘭 𝘌𝘯𝘵𝘳𝘦𝘱𝘳𝘦𝘯𝘦𝘶𝘳𝘴. In his article, he shares three ways my book can elevate your work:

1️⃣ From “begging bowl” to bold brand
2️⃣ Data vs. stories: fundraising game-changer
3️⃣ Policy advocacy as brand-building

Shout-outs to Gareth Price for this quote and Cool.org for this picture.

And another gem. ⤵

“I wish this book had existed when I started; maybe I’d have made fewer mistakes and built stronger foundations from the start,” said Rosalin Abigail Kyere-Nartey.  

“Do yourself a favor and get this book,” she says.

Who here is currently reading it?

💪🏽💛
posts.core_message: Kevin’s book is a practical, must-read survival guide for nonprofit fundraisers and social entrepreneurs that helps shift organizations from a 'begging bowl' to a bold brand by balancing data and stories and using policy advocacy to build stronger foundations.

posts.post_content:
𝙁𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝙛𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 isn’t a clickable catchphrase.

I invented the term for a reason.

Fundraising should be accessible vs. academic. And funding is the result of simple (repeatable) actions vs. complicated processes.

So if you boiled down my whole framework and book, and asked me to summarize the four most important steps in plain language, it’s this:

🎯 𝗕𝗘 𝗙𝗨𝗡𝗗𝗔𝗕𝗟𝗘
Craft a clear mission and vision +
Show that you’re achieving it

+

🔍 𝗕𝗘 𝗙𝗜𝗡𝗗𝗔𝗕𝗟𝗘
Say how you’re different +
Communicate well

=

💰 𝗚𝗘𝗧 𝗙𝗨𝗡𝗗𝗜𝗡𝗚


So yes, I’m thrilled my term caught on. But while we use the motto, let’s not forget the basics: Fundable & Findable™ is a method.

First the phrase.
Then the fundamentals.

💪🏽💛

P.S. Which is most important, do you think: fundable or findable?
posts.core_message: Fundraising success depends on a simple, repeatable method: be fundable (a clear mission and demonstrated impact) and be findable (distinct positioning and effective communication), so the catchy phrase 'Fundable & Findable' should point to practicing these fundamentals rather than replace them.

playbook.executive_summary: Kevin’s best LinkedIn work centers on identity-led, reform-minded posts that reframe nonprofit practice and end with a clear social/peer call-to-action. From 100 posts (avg engagement 463), the highest-return combos are: reframing + problem-solution structure + provocative hook + authoritative/passionate tone — especially when framed for fundraisers, nonprofit leaders or comms pros. Practical rule: prioritize medium-length text posts (300–800 chars), post early-week (Mon/Wed), and use 25–50 character hooks; these settings align with the platform-level peaks (Monday avg 683, Wednesday avg 643) and Kevin’s format sweet spot (text avg 539 vs carousel avg 345).

signature_moves.name: Identity Mirror
signature_moves.technique: Open with a provocative identity-facing statement that mirrors the reader (“Your X isn’t a Y”), then enumerate why that belief is damaging and finish with a binary choice or demand (adjust expectations / do X). Often uses list-format or numbered options and an identity appeal frame.
signature_moves.why_it_works: Speaks to social identity and consistency—readers instantly see themselves in the mirror, triggering motivated processing (identity-based persuasion). This leverages Cialdini’s Consistency and Social Proof (readers align with identity norms) and Heath & Heath’s idea of
signature_moves.template: Bright Ideas stick because they’re concrete and surprising). Empirically: identity_appeal posts (count=5) average 793 engagement; Kevin’s single top post using this move reached 3,134 engagement.

example_posts.hook: Your solo Communications Officer isn’t a unicorn.
Fundraising is tough if you’re not tough.

signature_moves.name: Passionate Mobilizer
signature_moves.technique: A short, emotionally charged opening (provocative statement or statistic) + a tight problem-solution body that builds urgency, capped by a direct social CTA (Share / Fund / Sign). Tone is high-energy/passionate to mobilize action.
signature_moves.why_it_works: Combines urgency and social-action cues to trigger both emotional arousal and social proof—Cialdini’s Scarcity/Authority are present via urgency and passionate framing; share CTAs exploit network cascades. Data: 'Passionate tone + CTA' combo (8 posts) averages 1,216 engagement with a max of 2,375.
signature_moves.template: Hook (provocative statement or stat). Short paragraph showing the damage/need. 3 quick bullets: what to do right now. CTA: Share/Act now.

example_posts.hook: Fundraising is tough if you’re not tough.
What has aid ever done for anyone?

signature_moves.name: Build-to-Reveal Educator
signature_moves.technique: Start with a short paradox or provocative opener, lead readers through staged evidence or steps (build), then land a concise revealing lesson (reveal). Often uses antithesis and repetition to increase memorability.
signature_moves.why_it_works: Exploits Kahneman’s System 1/System 2 gap: the gradual reveal holds attention and the final insight produces an 'aha' (Heath & Heath’s 'SUCCESs'—Simple, Unexpected, Concrete). Empirically: build_to_reveal structure (count=10) averages 701 engagement and is a common feature across the top posts (max 2,661).
signature_moves.template: Hook (short paradox). Step 1: evidence/example. Step 2: counterexample or contrast. Final line: the rule/insight you want remembered.

example_posts.hook: Donors don’t act because of your data.

signature_moves.name: Myth-Bust Persuader
signature_moves.technique: Lead with a strong statistic or myth statement, then systematically dismantle the myth with concise counter-evidence and close with a prescriptive alternative.
signature_moves.why_it_works: Myth-busting activates corrective information processing and benefits from surprise and contradiction—people share corrective frames to signal competence (social signaling). Data: myth_bust posts (count=2) average 1,073 engagement and include one of Kevin’s top-5 posts (1,994).
signature_moves.template: Myth statement (bold/stat). 3 bullets: why it’s wrong (evidence). One-line alternative: what to do instead.

example_posts.hook: I’ve seen 450 pitches in the past nine years.

signature_moves.name: Reframe Authority (Problem → Better Problem)
signature_moves.technique: Take a common assumption and reframe it (reframing), show the practical costs using problem→solution logic, then give an actionable alternative. Tone is authoritative but concrete.
signature_moves.why_it_works: Reframing changes the decision frame and increases perceived value (Kahneman & Tversky). Kevin uses reframing heavily (65 posts) and it’s the most common framing among top-10% posts (reframing appears in 6 of the top-10%). Reframing posts average 472 engagement; problem_solution structure averages 405 engagement and is present in 50 posts.
signature_moves.template: Common belief: X. But X is actually Y because… (evidence). The better way: 3 steps you can do tomorrow.

example_posts.hook: What has aid ever done for anyone?

viral_formula.summary: Reframe a common assumption with a provocative 25–50 character hook, use problem→solution or list structure, adopt an authoritative or passionate tone, target fundraising/advocacy topics, and finish with either no explicit CTA or a 'share this' CTA.
viral_formula.theme: Fundraising_strategy (4 of top-10), Advocacy_activism (3 of top-10), Leadership (1 of top-10).
viral_formula.opening_hook: Provocative statement (4 of top-10) OR question/statistic (combined 2 of top-10); keep hook length in 25–50 chars (78 posts in that bucket avg engagement 506).
viral_formula.structural_pattern: Problem_solution (4 of top-10) OR list_format/build_to_reveal (total 6 of top-10); these appear most frequently in top decile.
viral_formula.framing_technique: Reframing (6 of top-10, 60%) ± Contrast (3 of top-10).
viral_formula.length: Medium (300–800 chars) — top-10 includes several medium posts (e.g., 481, 575, 622); platform-optimized average length bucket has best balance of depth and scanability.
viral_formula.format: Text (text posts avg engagement 539 vs carousel 345); top posts are mostly text.
viral_formula.cta: None (6 of top-10) OR Share_this (4 of top-10). Note: 'call_to_action' content category overall averages 833 engagement (23 posts).
viral_formula.emotional_appeal: Aspiration (5 of top-10), Empathy (2), Pride (2). Aspiration and empathy predict higher sharing/virality.
viral_formula.rhetorical_devices: Antithesis/contrast, repetition/anaphora, enumeration (tricolon), rhetorical questions and metaphor — these appear across the top posts (antithesis + tricolon combo seen in 35 posts avg 559).

calendar_pattern.posting_frequency: Kevin posts ~5.6x/week (median 6/week). Maintain this cadence; frequency is already aligned with audience expectations.
calendar_pattern.best_days: Monday (16 posts, avg engagement 683) and Wednesday (17 posts, avg engagement 643) are highest-value days. Avoid Sundays (13 posts, avg 145) and Saturdays (10 posts, avg 246) unless repurposing evergreen content.
calendar_pattern.theme_rotation: Rotate themes across the week: Monday = high-impact advocacy/leadership/reframe long text (aim for identity appeal or passionate mobilizer); Tuesday = practical fundraising_strategy how-tos or event lists; Wednesday = visual_creative or medium build-to-reveal posts; Thursday = leadership/fundraising problem→solution or myth-bust carousels; Friday = curated resources / conference lists / inspirational posts. Reserve weekend slots for repurposed carousels or short reads.
calendar_pattern.format_mix: Prioritize text (61 posts, avg engagement 539) for reach. Use carousels selectively for richer, saveable resources — carousels have higher comment-to-like (0.217) and share-to-like (0.133) ratios despite lower raw average (345). Optimal post parameters: medium length (300–800 chars), hook 25–50 chars, text format, post Monday or Wednesday.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Slow openings reduce early attention—story_opening posts (4) averaged only 171 engagement versus median overall 229.

anti_patterns.pattern: Long Narrative Arc without a clear payoff (narrative_arc)
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Narrative_arc posts (2) averaged 164; Kevin’s audience favors tight problem→solution or list reveals rather than drawn-out storytelling (top posts showed short build-to-reveal or lists).
Storytelling_frame posts (4) averaged 156—audience prefers reframes and active prescriptions over purely reflective tales.
Imperative openings (12 posts) averaged 236 — calls that start with 'Do this' feel bossy and lower engagement compared with provocative/stat hooks (provocative_statement avg 495).
Link_in_comments CTAs (10 posts) average 294, underperforming compared to 'share_this' (18 posts avg 732) and even no-CTA posts (57 posts avg 435). Links reduce native sharing and traction.

audience_psychology.primary_identity: Pragmatic mission-driven practitioners: fundraisers, nonprofit leaders, and comms professionals who see themselves as accountable, impact-focused problem-solvers. (Data: fundraisers = 68 posts avg 365; nonprofit_leaders = 23 posts avg 518; comms_professionals = 6 posts avg 914.)
audience_psychology.emotional_triggers: 1) Pride — high-performing orgs and professionals take pride; pride posts (4) avg 1,146 engagement (strong share potential). 2) Empathy — human-focused asks (8 posts) avg 798 engagement. 3) Aspiration — forward-looking how-tos and frameworks (58 posts) avg 388 engagement. 4) Urgency — practical immediate-action language (21 posts) avg 450 engagement.
audience_psychology.content_preferences: They prefer reframes, concrete steps and ‘rules to follow’ (reframing count=65 avg 472; problem_solution count=50 avg 405; list_format count=26 avg 474). They favor medium-length, prescriptive text over long narratives or soft stories.
audience_psychology.sharing_motivation: Kevin’s audience shares to: 1) signal identity and values (identity_appeal posts avg 793), 2) spread practical resources or action asks (call_to_action category avg 833; 'share_this' CTA avg 732), and 3) amplify advocacy frames (advocacy_activism theme avg 1,493). The combination of moral signaling (pride/empowerment) and utility is the strongest sharing driver.
